narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid middle-income family background and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile stood out in some positive ways, like your LSAT score of 35, which is close to the typical student's score of around 37. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on past students' academic records and backgrounds, estimated an 82.1% chance that you would fail it— a threshold that led to your application's rejection to protect the school's outcomes. This prediction hinged most heavily on your academic performance in school, particularly your position in the lowest decile for grades in Year 3, which strongly signaled to the model a higher risk of bar exam struggles compared to peers who ranked higher. Your Year 1 grades, also in the lowest decile well below the average of around the 6th decile, reinforced this concern as the second-biggest factor pushing the prediction toward failure. While your LSAT score helped counterbalance things somewhat by nudging the odds slightly in favor of passing—since it's not far off from what successful students typically score— it wasn't enough to overcome those early academic hurdles. In contrast to typical admitted students, who often enter with undergraduate GPAs around 3.2 and stronger high school decile rankings, your 2.9 GPA and bottom-decile placements made your profile look riskier overall. The model saw these patterns as the strongest indicators because they've historically correlated with bar exam challenges in similar applicants. This isn't a judgment on your potential or effort, but a data-driven flag based on thousands of past cases. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, perhaps aiming for community college or targeted GPA improvement before reapplying. You might also explore LSAT prep to push your score higher, as even small gains there could tip the scales. Talking to an admissions counselor about bridging these gaps, or gaining relevant experience like internships, could strengthen your next application. With focused steps on those early academic markers, you can reshape how models like this one view your bar exam prospects.
